title: Macramé lampshade
subtitle: A pendant lampshade knotted in natural cotton cord on a metal ring, approximately 30 cm diameter
excerpt: A macramé lampshade is knotted onto a pendant ring frame using lark's head mounts and rows of square knots, with a spiral section at the lower edge for shape and texture. The finished shade diffuses light through the cord gaps and suits an LED or low-heat bulb fitting.
sourceNotes: Macramé lampshade construction is consistent across UK and US contemporary macramé instruction. The lark's head and square knot sequence described is the standard approach. Observation-led from guild and workshop teaching.

glossaryTerms.definition: A folded-cord mounting knot used to attach working cords to a ring, dowel, or other mounting element. Fold a length of cord at its midpoint, place the fold over the ring, and pull both tails through the loop. The knot grips securely and sits flat against the ring.

The fundamental macramé knot, formed by two half-hitches in opposite directions around a pair of carrier cords. The left cord crosses over first, then the right cord crosses over; alternating directions produces a flat, square knot. Consistently crossing the same side first produces a spiral instead.

A continuous sequence of half-hitches all made with the same working cord crossing over the carrier in the same direction. Unlike square knots, which lie flat, spiral half-hitches cause the knotting cord to revolve around the carrier in a distinctive twist.

content.text: A macramé lampshade is built outward from a pendant ring: cords are mounted to the ring with
and then knotted in rows of
descending from the ring. A section of

columns at the lower edge adds texture and shape before the cords end in a loose fringe. The project requires managing a large number of cords simultaneously and maintaining even knot tension throughout. These are the main skills that make this ADVANCED.

attrs.title: Electrical safety
attrs.body: Fit the finished shade with the lamp unplugged. Use LED bulbs only: natural cotton cord can scorch near hot incandescent bulbs.

items.name: Pendant lampshade ring, 30 cm diameter, with fitting attachment
Small lower ring, 20 cm diameter (optional, for shaping)
S-hook or hanging point to suspend the ring at working height

content.text: Step 1: Cut and mount the cords

Cut 40 lengths of cord each 200 cm long. Fold each length at its midpoint and mount to the pendant ring with a lark's head knot, spacing the knots evenly around the full circumference of the ring. 40 cords (80 working ends) around a 30 cm ring gives one knot approximately every 2.4 cm. Hang the ring at a comfortable working height from an S-hook or door frame.

Working in groups of 4 cords (2 carrier cords, 2 working cords), tie a square knot in each group approximately 3 cm below the mounting ring. In the next row, shift the groupings by 2 cords so each new group uses 2 cords from adjacent groups in the first row. This alternating arrangement creates an open net structure. Repeat for 3 to 4 rows of alternating square knots at 3 cm intervals.

Below the square knot section, knot each group of 4 cords into a spiral half-hitch column for 10 cm: tie half-hitches continuously with the left working cord over the carrier pair, keeping the direction consistent so the cord spirals. Trim all cord ends to an even length below the spiral section or leave as a loose fringe. If using a lower ring, gather the cord groups and wrap them around the lower ring with overhand knots before trimming.

items.problem: The square knots are different sizes and the net pattern looks uneven
items.cause: The knot tension was inconsistent between groups
items.fix: After each row of square knots, hold the shade at arm's length and check that all knots sit at the same height. Re-tie any knot that is significantly higher or lower before progressing to the next row.

items.problem: The spiral half-hitch section lies flat instead of spiralling
items.cause: The knotting direction changed mid-column, producing square knots rather than a continuous spiral
items.fix: Mark the working cord with a clip or piece of masking tape so you can identify which side must always cross first. Remove and re-tie from the point where the direction changed.
